# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Suggestions for movies that portray positive masculinity?

**Score:** 87
**Body:** My little brother and his best friend (both 15) have been saying some things recently that have me concerned that they’re falling down the super toxic masculinity rabbit hole that’s so popular nowadays. So, I was thinking about maybe putting together a movie list for when they come over to my apartment to watch to hopefully show some more positive examples. Things that I would describe as positive masculinity: emotional intelligence, respect, kindness, humility, vulnerability, strength (not necessarily physical), morality, selflessness, confidence, empathy, so on…

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lord of the Rings trilogy.

Princess Mononoke
  - The men in LOTR cry, have doubts, get scared and are the some of the best characters because of this

Courage is not the absence of fear, but the triumph over it
  - "Hey, 15-yo brother and friend, full of insecurities and hormonal confusion, come watch this Princess movie"

If you can sell that, you've won the war already 
2. Robin Williams in Good Will Hunting
3. Not a movie, but Ted Lasso has the healthiest approach to masculinity I have seen. 

For movies, try Hunt for the Wilderpeople or Peanut butter Falcon. 
4. Okay taking into account that they are 15;

* Dungeons & Dragons: Honour Among Thieves (2023). It's a truly heartwarming comedy with two male (and two female) characters in leading roles who both display a lot of positive traits - kindness, cleverness, confidence, and one male supporting character who just radiates empathy and wisdom. The movie is also *extremely* funny, had my brother in tears. Genuinely one of the best comedies I've seen recently, and it looks amazing too.
* Maggie (2015). A zombie apocalypse drama about a father and a daughter. Mind you it's a drama, more than it is an action or a horror, its core component is the dread of it all, and it's one of the best zombie movies I've ever seen. Arnold Schwarzenegger plays the father character very well, with all the love and burden the plot requires.
* School of Rock (2003). Another comedy with a main character that's a bit of a wannabe rockstar jackass (played by Jack Black), who takes a job as a teacher and really grows to love and respect his class. Great comedy all-around, but if the boys like rock or metal music, even better.
5. Not even joking -- Magic Mike and Magic Mike XXL
6. To Kill A Mockingbird (1962)
7. Project Hail Mary (in theaters, but highly recommend it for a future watch) 

The Book of Eli

The Lord of the Rings series

Everything Everywhere All at Once

To Kill a Mockingbird
8. Superman 

## 2. Trashy, erotic movies you enjoy

**Score:** 79
**Body:** I've been watching and enjoying a lot of erotic movies lately. Some horror, some drama, some comedy, all total trash. I've watched things such as: 

Eleven Days Eleven Nights (1987), All Ladies Do It (1992), Whore Hospital (1997), SS Experiment Love Camp (1976), Story of a Cloistered Nun (1973), and Nightmare Sisters (1988) to name just a few. 

What suggestions would you throw out? They don't have to be good movies. Just trash you enjoy. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wild things should be on the list! 
2. Showgirls is GOAT of this category.
3. I love Caligula (1979) so fucking much
4. Viva

The love witch 
5. Bound

Wild Orchid

Basic Instinct

Body of Evidence 

And God Created Woman
6. 9 1/2 weeks-Mickey Rourke and Kim Bassinger. Two Moon Junction- Sherilyn Fenn and Richard Tyson.
7. Body Double (1984)
8. Everything from Tinto Brass.
9. The Poison Ivy trilogy. Technically there are 4 but the 4th one isn't really in the same vein, and should definitely be skipped.
10. Ilsa Trilogy! 

## 3. If you’re don’t mind dark topics, watch The Coffee Table and go in BLIND.

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I won’t say anything more about it. It’s VERY dark, very funny in a “I shouldn’t be laughing at this” type of way, and very entertaining.

It’s not gonna be for everyone and you’ll know if it’s for you in the first half hour, but if it is it’s an absolutely bonkers ride full of so many emotions and may leave you feeling drained after in the way a good movie does.

If any of that sounds like your alley, go give it a watch. You can find it on Prime.

Oh, and expect to feel uncomfortable.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I have been recommending that movie every time I can for the last year. 
2. I really "enjoyed" this one. It's plays like a very dark version of a Raymond Carver short story. 
  - It’s good. I found it amusing. 
3. I’ve seen Happiness (1998) and Michael (2011). I bet I can handle this. (Unless it’s like a new Salo. I’ll never watch that.)
  - I’d say it’s a very close humour to Happiness, you’ll love it if you liked happiness.
    - Oh, I don’t know if I “liked” Happiness. More like “I guess that’s a movie that was legally made”.
4. I had a lot of very awkward laughing. 
5. 100%. Incredible film. Going in blind is essential to fully enjoying this film. 

The Coffee Table. That’s it. Watch it. Call me after you see it. 
6. It's a fun watch but for me it was so unrealistic that it was hard to suspend disbelief so more funny than disturbing.
  - I’m curious what parts did you find unrealistic? It’s really hard to talk about it without spoiling it, but i thought the way it depicted the anxiety and dread and the reasons why he does he does in the movie gets answered and is realistic to how some people would react in a situation like that.

## 4. The Verdict (1982), starring Paul Newman. I bought it today, and I'm watching it now. I remembered that it is an excellent movie, but since I hadn't seen it in many years, I felt the need to refresh my recollection of it. After that, I'm comfortable saying that I highly recommend that you do.

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Paul Newman plays a lawyer who is drunk virtually all the time, but now he has a great medical malpractice case and he refuses to settle it, even though he was offered over $200,000 to do so. 

Once he realizes how inadequate that settlement offer is, he decides that it must go to trial. He wants this, even if to do so would require him to stay sober, something that is very difficult for an alcoholic, such as he is, to do.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Paul Newman's bet role, I think.
  - That's clearly The Hustler
2. As a writer, I think about one scene in this movie a lot. 

When Newman goes to visit this comatose patient for the first time, he's completely disengaged. Checked out, half-drunk, not sure he can be bothered with taking her case. But then, even through the viewfinder, her humanity reaches him. His whole demeanour changes. It's the moment he realizes he still has some fight in him, and he can't let this stand. 

The whole thing is silent until the nurse comes and asks who Newman is. 

"I'm her lawyer."

Just a masterful scene on all fronts. 
3. I remember it being really good but somewhat depressing. Guess I'll watch it again.
4. I remember watching it in some class in HS and thought it was a fantastic movie. It's sad that you don't see that one anywhere online any more. 
5. Yes, thanks for the memories.
6. Bruce Willis and Jigsaw sitting behind Newman as he delivers that final statement. 
7. Robbed of the Oscar
8. I might’ve been too high when I saw it, but is there any chance that when he plays the arcade game at the beginning there’s a tune of Here Comes the Bride/the Bridal chorus song?
9. Written by David Mamet and directed by Sydney Lumet. It's Newmans film but two other pros not to be underrated. 

## 5. Time after time  (1979)

**Score:** 21
**Body:** H. G .Wells find out who is  Jack the Ripper and that he used H.G. time machine,  so H.G went to look for him in 1976 .This is very good movie even though  premise  is kinda weird. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That is one of my favorite adaptations of The Time Machine and has a great cast of Malcolm McDowell,  Mary Steenburgen, and David Warner.
  - Malcolm McDowell and Mary Steenburgen married after meeting during the filming. Her son, Charlie McDowell, has had a running joke that his mother is Andie MacDowell.
2. It’s my second favorite movie where Mary Steenburgen falls in love with a time traveler.
3. This movie is awesome and has some silly humor which I adore. (The McDonald's (a Scottish restaurant) order, the out of order phone booth that he reacts to with brief overwhelming grief then uses the pay phone right next to it, It's awesome. 

I also like how he reacts to the cringe moments and language of 1979, as if he's from the future too, namely their lunch date conversation. 

Great movie that holds up well and shows off San Fransisco. 


4. Alex from A Clockwork Orange playing a good guy is truly bizarre.
5. i think remember watching this as a kid. can't wait to check it out tonight

thanks
6. Such a fun movie and I really liked the time travel optical effect
7. This movie is awesome. Great stuff. 
8. Great film.  Shows what good acting, script and directing can do with a silly premise.
9. Saw it in the theater when it first came out. It was great

## 2. The mesmerizing Irish actress Saoirse Ronan turns 32 today. Happy birthday to her!

**Score:** 974

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

![gif](giphy|RWfo49t63KTuw)
2. The movies: The Lovely Bones (2009) , Atonement (2007), The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014), Hanna (2011), Mary Queen of Scots (2018), Little Women (2019) and The Outrun (2024)
3. Liked her in Lady Bird!
4. How come there are so many professional/top tier Irish actors? Anyone know? I mean it’s a small country.
  - Small country with a relatively high GDP per capita, long standing tradition of arts and music, English speaking but with an exotic accent, and a significantly higher than average amount of people with rare visual traits (red hair, intense blue and green eyes, freckles, pronounced cheekbones, extremely pale skin).

Basically a perfect storm for a population that punches above its weight class in entertainment

Centuries of generational trauma and pain also probably predisposes the population to creative endeavors to some degree as well.
    - Wait, we sound exotic, that's a first.
      - You certainly do to the two entertainment capitals of the world, Los Angeles and London.

The acting biz is all about standing out, and Irish people definitely stand out from the rest of the pack in a lot of ways
  - Basically, we all grow up with an appreciation for arts.
5. ![gif](giphy|cKmeEpXkWY8HWc2fF0)
6. omg i literally just watched lady bird again last night, she's so talented!! happy birthday to her.

## 3. In Candyman (1992), the bees were real, including the ones inside Tony Todd’s mouth. He agreed to do the scene only if he was paid $1,000 per sting. He was stung 23 times.

In today's money, that $1000 bucks is about about $2300. So yeah, you are not far off.
2. They used 200,000 real bees, mostly newborn so less violent, and he had a dental dam to prevent him from accidentally swallowing them. They used a pheromone that simulated a queen bee one to ensure mostly docility.

4. Where's my Aragorn anecdote where he breaks his foot kicking a helmet?

I mean it was a memorable scene but …
6. He was absolutely terrifying. Saw him at a Comic-Con and stopped dead / nearly wet myself. He made a point to come say hello lmao!

## 4. Happy 47th birthday to Claire Danes!! What’s your favorite character she played?

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Appreciation Post for Andrei Rublev

**Score:** 44
**Body:** Andrei Rublev was my final Tarkovsky film, and I don't think I've ever been more profoundly affected by a movie. I have deeply loved every movie of his, with Stalker & The Sacrifice being some of my favorite movies ever. The one factor that differs this from most of his movies is that the plot progresses tremendously with the story ranging for over 15 years. This is also Tarkovsky's longest work, but every second felt important to the plot.

The way the film dealt with faith, art and religion was so captivating. The final chapter 'The Bells' was spellbinding, with me almost shedding a tear in the last few minutes. The film made me question objective morality, the meaning of mercy through cruelty and if art is a choice or necessity for humanity. Theophanes considering God to be an idol of fear and Rublev believing God to be love is one of the best internal conflicts of the film.

What makes this movie really stand out for me is the way it perfectly balances internal conflicts with external war and chaos. People die like flies but Andrei takes his 'vow of silence' after one act that he considers spiritual crisis. The conflict of God vs the modern man was showcased with precision too, with Tarkovsky showcasing the power and faith narrative smoothly. This was Tarkovsky's sophomore film, but the cinematography and execution put me in awe with every frame being as immaculate as it was.

Two takeaway characters for me were Durochka and Boriska. Durochka, presented as the holy fool in the movie was a beautiful representation of pure and vulnerable heart of the nation amid chaos. After Andrei's despair and loss of hope for art, Boriska was what brought beauty back into Andrei's world. The whole bell sequence had me in the edge of my seat. After all that chaos, all that destruction, the bell scene was what restored the faith for art inside Andrei. The scene with Boris saying, "My father never told me the secret! He took it to his grave" was so so beautiful. The response by Rublev was also transcendental. Tarkovsky at his best. Cinema at it's finest, in my opinion.

The montage of colorful painting was also a magnificent ode to art and beauty. The only time I felt something similar was probably while watching 2001: A Space Odyssey. While Kubrick generated fear and curiosity in me with the whole Star Gate sequence, the colorful scene in this movie's end made me so hopeful about the necessity of art for human life. I couldn't help getting emotional and thinking that I'd never forget this moment. As the film progressed, everything kept getting better and the final 'horses in the rain' scene was the icing on the cake!

So, that was my initial reaction to Andrei Rublev and how I could only appreciate every scene of it. This was one of the few films I'd consider flawless and unforgettable. I haven't had much time to read other people's interpretations, so my perspective might not be complete. Please correct me if some of my interpretations were senseless and feel free to present your thoughts on the film. Any sort of feedback will be appreciated!

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As others commented, it was a very problematic film for him. Ideologically, wrapped up in other mumbo-jumbo, some objected to his portrayal of both Russia and Rublev. Of course, the film touched on sensitive subjects... there are sections of the film that resonate with the abuses of the Soviet era, too, and the fate of artists and art... 

And yes, the final chapter is absolutely demolishing me, too every time, no matter how prepared I am for it.

The boy sobbing in that vast field of mud, held by the old man... and those lines... It is perhaps the most poignant  visual metaphor for an artist, his/her art, and society.   *“You’ve created such a feast, such a joy for people. Why are you crying?”* he asks Boriska who is crying his eyes out in the sea of mud. 

I would dare quote Tarkovsky from  The Poet of the Cinema: *“The artist exists because the world is not perfect. Art would be useless if the world were perfect, as man wouldn’t look for harmony but would simply live in it. Art is born out of an ill-designed world.”*

Absolutely phenomenal finale for a phenomenal film that is utterly mesmerising.


  - You've worded it so well. Despite it being problematic, it will forever be a very dear movie to me. Such a phenomenal movie with such a beautiful third act.
  - That is an incredible quote. Tarkovsky resonates across decades 
2. As strange as this may sound to you, Tarkovsky received severe criticism for *Andrei Rublev*. Goskino authorities considered it anti-Russian, as they refused to accept how this renowned 15th-century monk could be so self-centered and individualist of a person. Such traits were just too modern, which means Western-like; in other words, anti-Russian.

However, Tarkovsky was quite aware of this. What those critics couldn't see, was how *Andrei Rublev* depicts *the very onset* of Russia (and thus, Europe's) spiritual decay. Tarkovsky's mandatory themes—fate, art, passion, the ubiquitous power of secularized culture, faith, loneliness, and so on—coalesce in some colossal, god-like poetic masterpiece in *Andrei Rublev*. The film is almost a manifest on the world's state of the art, much like Shakespeare did with *Hamlet*: acknowledging the present by scanning its first negative symptoms in the past.

Tarkovsky really admired how the icon painters didn't sign their works. Shunning the self was crucial to him, as only selflessness leads to a true experience of life, 'the absolute' in *Andrei Rublev*. Btw, he was quite into Pavel Florensky, so maybe you'd like to look into that.
  - It really is shocking to me! The scenes with people burning Bible did make me think that this might have received criticism, but it's shocking to know that people also negatively critiqued Rublev's character.
    - One of the main themes is how art and artists are used and often abused by the people in power for their own ends, so it’s no surprise the bigwigs weren’t fans. Anyway, probably Tarkovsky’s best
3. Andrei Rublev was my first Tarkovsky and I just watched it this week. Totally agree with everything you’ve said. It’s a masterful work. 

The only thing that didn’t make sense was ending on the horses in the rain. What did that signify to you? It felt kind of random but nothing in this film is random so it left me a bit perplexed 
  - > The only thing that didn’t make sense was ending on the horses in the rain. What did that signify to you? It felt kind of random but nothing in this film is random so it left me a bit perplexed 

Horses embody natural beauty and purity, freedom, earthly joy and the profoundest sadness. Two of Tarkovsky's influences provide insight into this: the donkey in Bresson's *Au Hasard Balthazar*, and [the nightmare of the horse beating](https://biblioklept.org/2012/09/10/raskolnikovs-horse-dream-dostoevsky/) in Dostoyevsky's *Crime and Punishment* (later apocryphally attributed to Nietzsche before he went insane). A good essay examining the symbolic import of horses in the latter [here](https://sites.northwestern.edu/nurprt/2023/12/05/humans-and-horses-in-crime-and-punishment/).
4. wonderful writing. Glad you enjoyed it so much. It's a treasure and I have watched once (or twice) a year since probably 2004 or something. It's the perfect movie for my two cents. Doesn't get better. I wish I spoke Russian because I bet there is another level of poetry in the words lost in translation

## 2. Nymphomaniac - Pale Fire

**Score:** 44
**Body:** Currently reading Nabokov's Pale Fire and it greatly reminds me of Lars Von Trier's Nymphomaniac.

The meat of Pale Fire is a (fictional) man giving a commentary on a poem written by a recently dead (also fictional) Poet. His interpretations are completely ridiculous and often just him using the text to tell personal stories.

I couldn't help but be reminded of Nymphomaniac. The film is structured as a woman telling a story to a man, as she tells her story he keeps interrupting with tangents and interpretations to the point where the woman he's telling the story to asks if he's even listening at one point.

I think overall both works of art are different and contain very different themes outside of this but both function as a sort of parody of the critic who's so self obsessed and obsessed with their own idea or interpretation of something that they entirely refuse to even engage with the text. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ve only watched Nymphomaniac once but as Pale Fire fan and Nabokov follower in general, I find your comparison quite valid and rich. I’d have to reacquaint myself with both works to comment further, but you’re definitely onto something.

## 3. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (April 12, 2026)

**Score:** 6
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **The Running Man (2025, Edgar Wright)**

This was a fun watch! Stephen King novel and a remake, a man joins a deadly (and rigged) reality game show in order to provide for his family. Glen Powell was well cast, although bit of overacting going on. Overall, I enjoyed it. **6.5/10**

**Persona (1966, Ingmar Bergman)**

I was slightly underwhelmed by this one. A nurse is the caretaker of an actress gone mute. Entirely in black and white, it felt a bit abstract and mostly artsy. The story of the sexual beach encounter was sure something. **5/10**

**Jagten / The Hunt (2012, Thomas Vinterberg)**

This film blew me away. Highly disturbing subject matter. Lucas, a kindergarten teacher wrongly accused of a crime he didn’t commit, was incredibly played by Mads Mikkelsen. As the case quickly spiraled into a full blown witch hunt, I felt so frustrated and upset for Lucas. Very emotional scenes and storytelling. I want to check out more Danish film! **8.5/10**
2. **Interstellar (2014, Christopher Nolan) — re-watch** — Is *Interstellar* a Nolan movie for people who don’t really like Nolan movies? The first time I watched it, I didn’t think very highly of the rest of Nolan’s work, and I thought it was one of his better works. Since then, I’ve come around on *Inception*, *Memento*, *Tenet*, and *especially* the *Dark Knight* trilogy (*The Prestige* still bores me). And now, revisiting *Interstellar*, I find it to be oddly disappointing. I really want to like it — I *do* really like it — but it feels like it could have been one of Nolan’s best works, and yet doesn’t quite make it there.

I think maybe the best way to discuss what does and doesn’t work well in *Interstellar* is to contrast it with *Inception*, another Nolan movie that has many of the same superficial strengths and weaknesses, but juggles them much better. Like *Inception*, *Interstellar* is undeniably the product of a towering imagination; once again, Nolan displays a remarkable ability to cram more unique ideas into one film than most filmmakers do in their whole careers. And in both works, he manages to do this while still keeping the storyline remarkably clean, coherent, and easy to follow.

So why does *Interstellar* not reach the same heights? I would point to two main reasons. Firstly, the over-reliance on expository dialogue to convey things. *Inception* was filled with exposition dumps, too, but they were almost all integrated seamlessly into the story, and they were almost entirely concerned with the fictional mechanics of dream-infiltration, so that it made sense for an experienced infiltrator to need to explain them to a new recruit. In *Interstellar*, much of the exposition is based on real-world physics concepts, and often ones basic enough that the highly-educated lead characters should all already know them, so that one gets the sense they’re speaking more to the audience than to each other. On the other hand, the most pivotal exposition dump of all, at the climax, has the opposite problem. The deductions Cooper makes about humanity’s future are so wildly beyond anything that could reasonably be inferred from what he’s established as knowing that it feels like we’re listening to Nolan holding our hand through his work, rather than to the character figuring things out.

Another issue is with the emotional hook. A lot of people, myself included, have described *Inception* as feeling a little too cold and emotionless, and *Interstellar*’s greater focus on character relationships feels almost like a direct response to that. The problem is that just because Nolan gives the relationship more *focus*, that’s not the same thing as giving them more *depth*. Even with all of the extra screen time devoted to seemingly relationship-building scenes, almost all of the characters still feel too vaguely-sketched to form a real emotional connection to, and the fact that Nolan is so much more clearly aiming to spark one just makes it all the more glaring.

I do still think that *Interstellar* is a really impressive work, and, for most directors, making a movie this good would probably be the peak achievement of their careers. But from a director who’s put out as many masterworks as Nolan has, it’s still a little disappointing to see something that had so much potential to be another, but still falls just short. **8/10**

**A Heart in Winter (1992, Claude Sautet)** — I liked this one a lot. Very different from what I expected based on the descriptions I’d read — less “overtly sentimental”, more “quietly intelligent”. Highly recommended. **8/10**

**The Third Man (1949, Carol Reed) — re-watch** — On my fifth viewing of this movie, and I’m still finding new things to appreciate in it.

**START OF SPOILERS**

One thing that intrigued me on this viewing was the subtle nuance in the character of Harry Lime. On past viewings, it’s sometimes seemed a little disappointing to me that, in a movie which is seemingly about Holly learning that real good and evil are more complicated than he thinks, Lime turns out to be such a pure, uncomplicated monster. And yet, watching it now, I realize that it’s hard to say how true that really is. It seems significant that Holly was apparently a close friend of Harry’s for decades, and yet Harry apparently never attempted to get Holly to participate in his crimes — or, not until he is effectively backed into a corner, anyway. One can assume that he did not see Holly as a likely or valuable accomplice — but then why work to maintain the friendship for so long in the first place? Then there is Anna, who insists that Harry’s helping her to avoid deportation was done “out of kindness”. The movie never confirms that she’s right about this, but it never suggests any other motive he could have had for doing it, either. In his dialogue with Holly on the Ferris wheel, he says that seeing the people far beneath them from a distance as “little dots” would make Holly less horrified by the idea of killing them for money, and it seems like he really believes it. I can’t help but wonder if this is a kind of veiled autobiography on Lime’s part; perhaps he still remembers a time when he wanted to be the hero as Holly does, until he discovered how much easier theft and murder became when the deaths happened somewhere he didn’t have to see them.

**END OF SPOILERS**

However you interpret it, the movie remains basically perfect. **10/10**

**Movie of the week:** *The Third Man*
3. [**WEEK # 275:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

I resisted seeing the Bosnian award-winning  **QUO VADIS, AIDA?** (2020) all these years, but I finally succumbed. I can't keep absorbing the never-ending deluge of atrocities, genocides, abuse and cruelties that are flooding our lives. On the other hand, I must. 

It tells of the Srebrenica massacre during the Bosnian war of the 90's, and especially of one woman, a translator for the UN forces, as she tried to save her family. It's brutal, authentic and horrifying, depressing in the sense of powerlessness that everybody (except the killers) has. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ErLD8P4VUjY) *[Female Director.]*

🍿 

**CITY OF LIFE AND DEATH** (2009) is a similarly disturbing exposition about an even larger atrocity, the Nanjing Massacre of 1938. My first by director Lu Chuan, of the Chinese "Sixth Generation". It's an epic, chaotic war movie done in incredible black & white, depicting an endless barrage of brutalities, executions, mass rapes and murder. The battle scenes are as elaborate as 'Saving Private Ryan', and all in all it's a movie that should be on the list of all followers of this harrowing genre. It's not an experience for the squeamish. The film received some domestic backlash because it depicted all its characters in a sympathetic light. [The trailer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KGvrEIIQDj8). The full film is available for free on YouTube.

I plan on following up these two with 'The Painted Bird' and 'All quiet on the western front', but maybe not right away.

🍿 

**STRONGROOM** is a re-discovered British B-thriller from 1962 about a heist gone wrong. During a bank robbery on Easter day, three amateur crooks lock a bank manager and his secretary in the safe ("Strongroom"), but later realize that they must return to free them, or they'll suffocate in the airtight vault. It's a suspenseful nail-biter, and the race against the clock is tense and well scripted. The newly-remastered BFI copy is cited by Scorsese, Tarantino and Edgar Wright. 

The premise was repeated in 'No Country for Old Men' when the Josh Brolin character ("Llewelyn") decides to go back to the scene of the crime with similar disastrous consequences. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7K_Row0Rbxo)

(By complete coincidence, I also watched it on Easter day, 64 years later).

🍿 

Another random pick which **immediately became my favorite discovery of the week:** 
The Oscar-nominated **MUDDY RIVER**, a debut feature by (unknown-to-me) Japanese director Kōhei Oguri. It's a masterful story of childhood, about two 8 yo boys who become friends in post-war Osaka. One is the son of a poor family who runs a noodle-shop on the bank of the river, and the other an even poorer one, who lives on a run-down houseboat with his sister and widowed, prostitute mother. It's an innocent, moving, Neo-realist Children Film as fine as the best of them. It was shot in 1981, but feels like a mid-50's black and white Ozu. **10/10**.

🍿 

**THE ALPINIST** (2021) is a terrific documentary about Marc-André Leclerc, a young Canadian free-solo rock climber who made a name for himself by climbing some of the most challenging summits in the world. Bold and unafraid, he maintained a spiritual purity, even as he overcame impossible, non-human obstacles. He comes across as one of a kind inspirational figure, a genuine free-spirit hippy who lived his life to the fullest. But of course he was also crazy.

As much as I don't care for physical adventures at all, this film was tense, engrossing and beautiful. The filmmakers specialize in rock climbing documentaries, and have produced two dozens similar films. Still, it's hard to imagine how they got the footage and close ups, especially way up on top of these remote peaks. (Must be drones, right?). 

Spoiler alert: [He didn't make it from his last climb.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_2j0qCsMQmo)

🍿 

**INVITATION TO THE DANCE** (1956) was the first film that Gene Kelly directed alone, without Stanley Donen. It's a second-rate anthology, consisting of 3 unrelated stories. The 'Children of Paradise' segment, and the modern Jazz story based on 'La Ronde' were forgettable. The 3rd part ('Sinbad') was better, in that Kelly was paired with a good kid sailor, as well as a crew of Hanna-Barbera animated creatures. The wordless film features only dance and pantomime, but no dialog. 3/10. 

🍿 

**LOUDER THAN BOMBS** (2015), my 6th drama by Norwegian Joachim Trier, in his first English-language film. It's a slow-burn Scandinavian-style art film, a small & quiet family relationship story with four characters.

Isabelle Huppert was a famed war photographer who died 3 years earlier in a car accident that was actually a suicide. Her husband and their two sons are all trying to come to terms with their complicated interactions and less than perfect lives. It's beautiful, artful - but forgettable. I only watched it for Isabelle Huppert, because she was born exactly 6 weeks before me, and because I love to watch her face. 5/10. 

🍿 

**PABLO BERGER X 2:** 

🍿 Re-watch ♻️: **ROBOT DREAMS**, a heart-warming love letter to 1980's NYC, with exquisite details and Earth, Wind & Fire's ''September' on repeat. And all that with zero dialogue. A nerdy East Village dog assembles a robot to combat his loneliness. The 2023 Oscar nomination was well-deserved and belonged to Berger as much as to Sara Varon, the original animator of the characters. 9/10.

🍿 **ABRACADABRA** (2017) is my 3rd by Spanish Pablo Berger. While 'Robot Dreams' was absolutely delightful, his first feature 'Torremolinos 73' was woefully misfired. But this low-brow comedy about ghosts is so much worse.  It had a promising 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes, but it was just terrible. 

A nasty husband and father is undergoing a supernatural personality change when he lets himself be hypnotized as a prank at a wedding. His split personality struggles with memories of a dead schizophrenic mass-killer who sometimes appears as a chimpanzee, Etc. Etc. The absurdist plot and bland acting offer nothing of value. It seems that 'Robot Dreams' was a one-off. **1/10.**

*"The sun is really dying, isn't it?"*

**PROJECT HAIL MARY** (2026) got such positive acclaim that I was really looking forward to seeing it. Universally praised as the 'next big wholesome thing', an ode to friendship and hope and wonderment, it's exactly what we all need at the moment. 
Boy, was I disappointed. Knowing nothing about real "Science", I need to be convinced that when a piece of entertainment is trying to sell me some big scientific explanations they are credible. '2001' and 'Close encounters' for example, both of which were fictional and both are referred to here, also had to use words in order to explain their plots, but you never doubted why and what had happened there. That Ryan Gosling dude was a high-school science teacher, so he should Science, Bitch! With gritted teeth, I lasted for one tortuous hour before I had to bail out. ⬇️ Could Not Finish ⬇️.

**CODE RUSH** (2000) is a PBS documentary about the last year of Netscape, as it releases its source code for free, and before it was bought by AOL. Life of the 24/7 software engineers in Silicon Valley at the turn of the century was hectic, as the culture of corporatism planted the seeds for everything that is happening today. I used to follow this world closely, so it was interesting to re-meet the Po Bronson's, and Bill Gates', and Marc Andreessen (before he developed his giant egg-shaped, fascist head) and Jamie Zawinski (whose blog I still follow today), Kara Swisher, Etc.

4. **Rise of the Planet of the Apes** (2011) \*\*\*\* A compelling drama that ends with a surprisingly well-done action sequence on the Golden Gate Bridge. I would have liked a little more focus on Lithgow's character, but it's great watching the apes gain intelligence and the ape acting is fantastic. They're so much more expressive than the human actors (modern film acting is far too restrained). It also has a terrific ending that sets up the sequels. I didn't expect much and came away impressed with the solid directing. 

**Dawn of the Planet of the Apes** (2014) \*\*\*\* The confusingly titled "Dawn" is the most solid film in the series, a full-on ape movie with effects so good that you just take it for granted. You can't see the human under the digital makeup, but you can see the acting. It also builds to an action climax that's the best in the series, an impressive confrontation between man and apes. It's better than it has any right to be, and the Muir Woods setting is gorgeous.

**War for the Planet of the Apes** (2017) \*\*\*\* "War" tries to up the scale but ends up being the weakest film, if only because it borrows ideas so liberally from *Apocalypse Now*, and Woody Harrelson is no Marlon Brando. The emotional manipulation is also more obvious. That's not to say it's a bad film, and it does expand the world and bring a satisfying conclusion to Caesar's story. 

**Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes** (2024) \*\*\*\* After a break for COVID, "Kingdom" takes us many generations later where the events of the earlier films are just legends, and we have a Mad Max dystopian ape world that could go on forever (and the ending begs for more). I like how they incorporate the remnants of civilization and would love to see them expand on that. The apes talk a lot more in this one, and their Tarzan-like delivery can be tiring. It was better and more expressive when they used sign language. The emotional manipulation is more blatant and it has a more TV series feel, perhaps because of all the talking, but it also offers the best villain of all the movies, and yet another impressive action sequence with the flood.
  - The FX in Dawn are some of the best FX for main characters ever, hand down, especially up that point in cinema. I need to revisit it and see how they hold up, but your comment seems to confirm that they have held up.


I'll have to go watch Kingdom. Thanks for sharing!
    - I don't think any digital character has topped Gollum, but Caesar comes close, and the effects are flawless.
5. **What Happened Was (1994, dir. Tom Noonan)**

A must-watch for Charlie Kaufman fans. He didn't have any direct involvement with the film, but he regards it as one of his favorite films, director and star Tom Noonan was a frequent collaborator with Kaufman, and What Happened Was touches on themes about communication and connection that Kaufman would explore in movies like Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, Anomalisa, and I'm Thinking of Ending Things. What Happened was is a stripped-down, narratively complex story about two characters enduring a first date that can't seem to go right. Thematically, the film centers on the way people relate to each other and the world at large, contrasting Karen Sillas's detached curiosity with Noonan's sterile cynicism. Their performances channel so much awkwardness and vulnerability, and along with the sharp dialogue turn what could have been an interminable ninety minutes into a soulful, nuanced picture.

**Joan the Woman (1916, dir. Cecil B. DeMille)**

DeMille's attempt to break into the American epic genre DW Griffith had pioneered is largely successful. DeMille has always been more skilled with a camera than a pen, and this film is gorgeous and full of thrillingly chaotic medieval battles. He captured the scope of the Hundred Years' War admirably, making each scene appropriately majestic and entertaining in equal measure. His use of visual effects, pretty uncommon in his earlier features, is also commendable. The holy apparitions, particularly the glowing swords, look excellent, and the use of colored fire in the tragic climax was inspired. We're still a long way off from Technicolor, but DeMille's technique is still impressive and effective at grounding the viewer.

Narratively, Joan the Woman runs into a few problems. It tells the story of Joan of Arc, famed saint and military leader, and takes strange liberties with her story. DeMille invents an English soldier named Eric Trent, with whom she falls in love in the film's first act. They meet in an English raid on Joan's village, where Trent promises that he won't rape and murder her, which is enough for her to fall in love with him. Typically, a story about people from opposing sides in a conflict would be about how war is wrong and diplomacy and love are good, but Joan's call to arms is unaffected by her relationship with Trent. She hacks and slashes through Englishmen like the best of them. I get that they're an invading force, but thematically it doesn't jive well with her affair with an Englishman. This surely stems from an attempt to appeal to French nationalists. At this time, the Germans were holding on by the Marne and shredding through allied soldiers. It makes sense to make a movie set during one of France's proudest military moments where a Christian daughter of France repelled invaders, but you can't just make a movie about Joan killing English people. The British Expeditionary Force is in France, killing and dying so Paris is safe. So here Joan and Trent represent France and the UK coming together... while France fights England. It's very strange, but the intentions are noble.

As muddled as Joan the Woman's story is, I like it a lot more than the typical DeMille narrative. It moves at a steady pace and the characters are well-written and well-performed. Geraldine Farrar's Joan is a little ephemeral but very tenacious, Wallace Reid's Trent has a strong arc about realizing the virtues of mercy, and Raymond Hatton's Charles VII transitions well from a sympathetic petty king to a nebbish monarch who's too entrenched in politics to stand up for the little people who upjumped him to a place of legitimacy. It's a decently complex narrative that, with a little work, could have become genuinely great. As it is, aided by wonderful action and composition, Joan the Woman is a very impressive and worthwhile film.

**Resident Evil: Afterlife (2010, dir. Paul WS Anderson)**

In his thrilling return to the director's seat, Anderson course corrects the Resident Evil cinematic franchise. Gone are the vague game adaptations and Mad Max rip-offs. Here's where the series should always have been: discount Wachowski schlock. The story of Afterlife is totally stupid but Anderson's adroit use of special effects, lousy gunkata, and embarrassing imagery from the games make this a total blast. You can tell plain as day that Anderson has just given up, and it's weirdly endearing. I don't want to pay Afterlife any genuine compliments, but I will recommend it for a bad movie night. Sometimes you just need entertainment, and this supplies it in spades.

## 4. How do you define a good story in a movie?

**Score:** 1
**Body:** As I have watched more movies, I have realized that a compelling story matters a lot more to me than other factors that make a movie technically excellent. However, I've been having trouble defining this "goodness." 

Is it high stakes? Memorable characters? Relatability? 

In my head, I've been coming back to The Social Network as an example of a movie I personally enjoy that has an excellent story; while so much else about it is great, I feel that everything that is working in that movie is so good because it's ensuring a great story. The score is great at communicating how different characters feel, the dialogue ensures that the information delivery is fast yet pleasurable to consume. 

Still, I'm having trouble being denotative about what makes its story so good. 

What are your thoughts?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think Social Network (a movie I also love) is a very illustrative example, because it shows that you’re barking up the wrong tree: that is *not* a movie with a particularly interesting story. It’s a biopic about the founding of Facebook! And it’s a pretty loosely structured one: the big structural overlay is Zuckerberg’s dueling lawsuits, but it doesn’t really get into how these resolve. There is nothing inherently very interesting about that story. It certainly doesn’t make me want to go out and buy *The Accidental Billionaires*.

The things that actually make Social Network good are the things you identify—the score and the dialogue—as well as the cast. The cool thing about that movie to me is the way all these elements come together. I usually find Aaron Sorkin’s scripts mawkish, and David Fincher’s anhedonic-thriller directorial style sort of flat. But they made an *amazing* collaborative team, especially when mediated by Reznor/Ross and by Jesse Eisenberg (who can pull off both Sorkin’s screwball patter and Fincher’s silenty-staring-out-the-window bits and give a little depth to them).

When I think of “good story” in a movie, in its purest form, I’m usually thinking of old Hollywood. Casablanca, The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance, The Apartment. Last week I watched “To Be Or Not To Be” and that was a *perfectly* constructed story, in a way you rarely see anymore. These movies are complex, satisfying, every scene matters, the progression of events is 100% interwoven with the progression of the characters.
2. It's a combination of everything you stated. Setting up stakes, making the characters feel real, keeping the subject matter interesting. It all builds together.

One contrast of two movies with good stories is Lord of the Rings and Whiplash. They couldn't be further apart in subject matter. The stakes in LOTR are very hollywood, end of the world type stuff, whereas Whiplash is very personal and focuses on the ambition of one character. In this light, stakes for the viewer seem really important.

But then if you look at something like Eraserhead, the stakes are less pressing in that way. The story takes on an atmosphere, and the narrative becomes more of an exhibition of fears, stress, etc. Of this new father. There is no neat wrapping up of the story details, but it is engaging and fascinating in the way it comes together. It relies more on vignettes than standard narrative Devices. While to some it may be off-putting in the way its tells its story, it engages and challenges standard ideas of storytelling. It's art like this which lets you see how the boundaries of conventional filmmaking are illusory. 

I could go on giving contrasting examples all day, but I think to summarize my idea, the characters, subject matter, or events of the story have to be interesting and communicated effectively. The best movies will make a character feel real, even when the setting is fantastical. You can have some of the pillars of a good narrative without the others, and the best stories will lean heavily into the concepts that most specifically fit their narrative.
3. A good story engages you and makes you anticipate how it will unfold. If you're asking these questions, you're engaged:

* Good plot... "What's going to happen next?"
* Good character... "What do they think of that?"
* Good theme... "What does this mean?" 

Ideally, you're asking all three at the same time.
4. I'm sure there are more academic methods, but for me it really comes down to how many of the elements that are set up, actually pay off down the road. These payoffs can be for the literal narration, or to help build the thematic concepts the movie is hoping to instill. Something I also look for is whether or not those thematic ideas are supported by the literal narrative and vice versa. Again, it's about having the ideas expressed actually come to fruition down the line.

Characterizations, sub plots, world building etc. can all fall to the wayside if those details don't actually add to the experience of watching the movie. 

It's hard to be "objective" at times when some stories really connect with you and some don't, but looking at things like this has helped me assess some movies from a third party perspective. Even when I haven't really loved a movie, I'm able to notice the elements that serve a function and find a lot of value in works I may have not really gelled with. 

Not to say there's no subjectivity to it, but it becomes less about what I enjoyed, and more about what services what the movie is trying to do. Different movies are hoping to accomplish different things, and for me this has helped assess each movie on its own merits.
5. In addition to the things said below, lately I've been getting into movies with stories that are as possible. Think Predator, Alien, Pulp Fiction, and A New Hope.

I know Predator and Alien is comparatively low brow, but they illustrate my point well. Each of the three movies show things happening rather than relying on exposition. Each has very clear conflicts, both with the protagonist and antagonist and with the protagonist and their allies. 

Most importantly, the protagonist has to make decisions in each of these threads, and that's what shows us their character. Pulp Fiction does this perfectly. Bruce Willis makes decisions almost every frame he is in, and by the end of it, we feel like we know him. The second Matrix movie has the protagonist make exactly one choice in 140 minutes of runtime. If we took the second Matrix in isolation, Neo would have no personality at all. 

I'd seriously recommend watching some movies that are notorious for *poor* storytelling. Watch a movie like The Room with a notebook, and reflect on why the story makes no sense. Likewise, Battlefield Earth is a masterclass in pisspoor storytelling. 
6. There’s really not a one size fits all definition, except in the broadest sense that a story is good if the thing I’m watching on screen right now is engaging and I care about what’s happening, and ultimately that’s a subjective criteria. I’ve seen people criticize films that I think have great stories for being boring with nothing happening, and I’ve criticized acclaimed classics for having boring stories where nothing happens. This weekend I watched Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy. There are smart critics who said it’s a brilliant story with unpredictable twists, political intrigue, and memorable side characters. There are also smart critics who said the story is convoluted and confusing and drags down an otherwise well acted and crafted film. Neither is “wrong”, what counts as “good” writing is subjective.

I don’t think it’s possible for anyone to come up with any single specific definition that you couldn’t poke dozens of holes in with counter examples. Referencing your bullet points: Richard Linklater has made many great films, most of which have extremely low stakes, but his characters are engaging enough that the story can essentially just be “character has a life experience and grows up” and it works. Contrast with: Parasite, JFK, and Inception to name three are brilliantly plotted thriller tapestries where scenes build on each other to create tension and elements intertwine to make you look at earlier events a different way by the end of the film, but I don’t think any of those have particularly memorable characters, they’re all pretty one-note and plot functional and that works for the stories being told. Most of the great studio films of the 70s have characters that are memorable but purposely aren’t relatable or likable, those traits would work against dark stories like Chinatown or A Clockwork Orange or Network. 

The Social Network probably can be lumped into that last group, on paper the meat of the story is a friendship dissolving because a rich asshole becomes even more of an asshole as he gets even richer. It’s not a traditionally compelling story, because there’s not really any tension over whether Facebook will succeed as a product and anyway you don’t want it to succeed as a product because Mark Zuckerberg sucks. It doesn’t have likable characters, save for 2 minutes of Rooney Mara, and the central conflict of being screwed over by your business partner probably isn’t relatable to the audience. It‘s not historically accurate, to rule out another possible definition of a good story as something informative of real events. But Mark Zuckerberg is an interesting movie character, he has plenty of specific quirks that differentiate him from generic rich asshole villains. He even has charm in a superficial way that the audience sees through before the characters do, almost like a horror movie. Most of the scene to scene tension comes from wondering how morally low he can sink, surely there must be a bottom somewhere, until he ultimately throws Eduardo out and you realize there truly is no limit. All of this is happening at 90 miles per hour because the characters don’t stop talking for even a moment, which is the normal Sorkin thing and kind of divisive in other movies but it works here because Zuckerberg is always talking like he’s trying to sell his scene partner on something, and that feels true to the character. 
7. Great question. I was saying last week that this was the kind of topic that doesn’t gain traction on this subreddit, I guess you’re proving me wrong.

I also think that one of the best ways to talk about it is to go straight to examples of what you think defined a good story for you, as you’ve done.

We as viewers, I would argue, are always learning about ‘the possible’ in film storytelling in the current year when we see the new films of the best contemporary directors, and if that is not true, then we can observe that “films aren’t as good as they used to be” or at least something like that “films aren’t as narrative as they used to be.” I don’t know if I’m getting ahead of myself when I say this though. (I don’t dislike contemporary films at all, and depending on if I’ve seen some good contemporary films recently, I may well argue that films are as good now as they used to be or ever were.)

To address the thought that films are less narrative nowadays, that they typically tell less story than films in the past, I’ll name two films I saw in the cinema yesterday, Sirat and Father, Mother, Sister, Brother.

I guess Sirat could be compared to a film from the past like Antonioni’s The Passenger, but I feel like even in that film the main character travels a bit more of an arc from the story’s beginning to end than the main characters of Sirat. 

But I think that Sirat might also even be compared with soap opera style stories of runaway sons or daughters like Bangkok Hilton or Return to Paradise. Return to Paradise is a melodrama from 1998 that harkens back at least 20 more years to Midnight Express. Compare those two films and it seems like American mainstream cinema’s approach to storytelling didn’t noticeably change in twenty years.

In those twenty years mainstream Hollywood film was a comfortably popular medium. New stars could be made. Claire Danes and Joaquin Phoenix, young stars in Return to Paradise, are still well known names today. I believe that film critics like Siskel and Ebert tended to evaluate films in those years almost entirely on **how successfully they thought they delivered an engaging story**.

Cinema has since then become a somewhat more niche interest. I believe films have become less narrative. Now critics write of Father, Mother, Sister, Brother,

>Writer/director Jarmusch has called “Father Mother Sister Brother,” which he wrote in three weeks, an “anti-action film,” but if you’re looking closely enough or tuned in to its hangout-movie sensibility, it has more action than most bona fide action movies, even when much of the action here is offscreen, under-the-surface, unsaid.

Anyway, so far I haven’t really tried to name the elements of what makes a good story to me.

There are different kinds of stories that appeal to me. Robert Bresson is one of my favorite filmmakers. Sometimes there is an abundance of narrative content in his films, such as Au Hasard Balthazar, in which the main character, a donkey, is owned by half a dozen different owners throughout, and is a vehicle to look into the stories of all of those individuals, and at the same time Marie, daughter of his first owners, grows up and experiences the world in a parallel way to Balthazar. At other times, Bresson’s stories vividly illustrate one corner of life in a small town, such as the adaptations of George Bernanos, Diary of a Country Priest and Mouchette. These films are about as full of content as their sources. Others of his films, particularly The Trial of Joan of Arc, is more minimal, but the stories of his films don’t ever strike me as not having contained enough content in order for me to get a full understanding of the characters or the world which they are exploring.

That said, Bresson does represent a kind ‘less story, less elaboration’ approach, with some of his films having very short running times, main characters played blankly by non-actors, extremely functional dialogue, and so on.

On the other hand, I like epics of various kinds. I think of some popular nineties long movies as, regardless of anything else, examples of pure narrative that is audience appealing. Films such as Forrest Gump, The Shawshank Redemption, Titanic, Saving Private Ryan. Equally, the Altmanesque Boogie Nights and even Heat, also Gosford Park by Altman himself.

In contemporary foreign language movies I also often like when films have similarly lengthy and dynamic stories. A good recent example is The Delinquents (2023). This 190 minute film meanders, but it has a solid narrative engine, which is a bank robbery. Certain plot-types are inherently narrative, such as a bank robbery. In the case of The Social Network, the law suit is a structuring device, while the engine is the notion of a betrayal or of ‘making enemies’.

A road movie can be another narrative engine. However simply the exploration of a unique character in some kind of given context can be enough of a narrative engine. Shortly before I saw The Delinquents in 2024, I saw a film that I think stands somewhat beside it as a long contemporary art house film that is fun and compelling: Don’t Expect Too Much From the End of the World. It’s not a road movie so much as a ‘day in the life’ of a woman who works at a low level in the Romanian film industry and is on the road in Bucharest and its surroundings all day. The Delinquents and Don’t Expect Too Much fold in various whimsical or inventive elements which a plot description could never really adequately hint at or prepare you for. Above all, I appreciate this sense of discovery while watching a film.

To try to bring some of what I’ve said already together. I like different filmmakers’ approach to story. I typically appreciate a narrative that is long and varied and compelling. But I also said I like Bresson even when he’s very minimalist. That might be because I think that Bresson is trying to also make an argument or justify his worldview through his stories. They have a payoff, as though they’ve proven a problem, or something. (The problem of Une Femme Douce is ‘why did this young married woman commit suicide?’) 

Of course, longer and varied movies can perform a similar operation, being not just a string of novelties, but having internal coherence, and adding up to the presentation of a worldview that contains the argument for that conclusion within or given the content of the story. Getting such a sense from the end of a film is always a good thing. I just think that it’s all the more good, when the preceding story has touched on a lot of different themes or aspects of life, often through depicting characters’ journeys which have taken them on a wide arc. Do you think that this sounds like, for instance, The Godfather I and II, which have long been cited as standard bearers of great storytelling?
8. I think there are things that have to be in place first before a story can come alive. Maybe I can describe them as 'world building'. World building can be internal (the characters' inner lives) or external (the environment of the film). In the best films it's both. Once world building is established, the story can happen. 

Personally I engage with stories that communicate ambiguity. I have to be convinced by them (and the world building contributes greatly to that), but I also have to not fully understand them right away. Ambiguity can be found anywhere - moral ambiguity, emotional, psychological, political; all are a challenge, and challenge is engaging. 

Off the top of my head, an example where all these elements are very strong is Taxi Driver. Both the internal world of the characters and the external environment they exist within are tremendously vibrant and characterful, and the literal narrative is underpinned by a profound moral ambiguity. It's one of the most intensely engaging stories I've ever experienced. 

A more lightweight example might be something like The Devil Wears Prada. Hathaway and Streep successfully establish their characters' internal experience - the external world is solid - and the ambiguity presents itself in the central character's implicit hypocrisy (is Andy correct to sacrifice loyalty for the sake of ambition?) and also in the world of the film (is fashion frivolous and superficial or is it an important industry based on genuine creative genius?) 

This can apply to abstract or absurdist films also. Memoria (2021) constructs a subtle, multi-dimensional, highly complex, and yet in its way coherent world (both internal and external) within which a narrative full of contradiction and mystery unfolds. If the world building is strong enough, the ambiguity can be as simple as 'WTF is going on?' (Of course the ultimate example of this is David Lynch.)
9. I actually thought about this exact question a while ago and tried to write down what specifically makes a story "good" to me.

Multi-dimensional characters & authentic dialogue: Characters can't just be purely "good" or "evil." They need a past, real flaws, and deep motivations. Even in a fantasy setting, their actions have to feel human. Their dialogue has to sound authentic and fit their personality, which is exactly what Sorkin does so well.

A lived-in world & real consequences: Whether it's an elite Harvard club or an alien planet, the setting needs to be immersive. And more importantly, the characters' actions must have logical, lasting consequences that actually impact the world around them.

Strong pacing & structure: A story needs a clear dramaturgy and pacing that knows when to build tension and when to slow down so a scene can really breathe.

Thematic depth & subtext: A great story is more than just its plot. It has thematic coherence (a core theme that runs through the whole movie) and doesn't spoon-feed the audience. It uses subtext that you have to decode yourself.

Multiple perspectives & ambiguity: I love it when a story shows different viewpoints and leaves room for interpretation instead of forcing a specific moral on you.

Emotional resonance & food for thought: Ultimately, a good story has to hit you emotionally (whether that's joy, grief, or comfort) and leave a lasting impression that makes you think long after the credits roll.

To your point about The Social Network: it works so well because it perfectly hits all these marks without you even noticing the mechanics behind it.

## 5. The downfall of Abdellatif Kechiche: How his greatest triumph, Blue Is the Warmest Color,  led his downfall

**Score:** 2
**Body:** How do you adapt a Lesbian graphic novel, which was extremely political in its content, and turn it into a fetishistic soft-core lesbian male fantasy? 

The book is a lot better than the movie with a more haunting and powerful ending. Kechiche was not interested in any of the politics, the Lesbian aspect seemed to only be there to fulfill his fantasies. 

  
The fact Lea Seydoux, and to a lesser degree Adele Exarchepoulous, came out to say they were uncomfortable by the way they were directed and Kechiche responded by attacking Lea said it all. 

I had been already disturbed by another movie of his called Black Venus. He deserved the artistic banishment he received. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He's finished for other reasons. He went bankrupt and he suffered a stroke a year ago, that prevents him from speaking, at least for the moment. Hence the nearly surprise release of Mektoub, My Love: canto due a few months ago in France, after years of being mentioned but never seen. It was apparently mostly shot in 2018. It was completed by his editor, apparently with his instructions, but it's very pedestrian compared to his previous standards.

This second part introduces a new subplot that quickly takes over the rest of the narrative. The lead character Amin and his cousin befriend an American star and her producer husband who stay at the place. They become quickly interested in a sci-fi script he had written as a possible vehicle for her. And the cousin fancies her. This sidelines all the other characters from canto uno.

The whole plot rings completely hollow. The American star is supposed to be both mesmerizing and full of energy, but the actress has a limited range, the whole glamour stuff is ridiculous (the rich couple doesn't even have body guards, servants, etc. because Kechiche couldn't afford too many extras). And the actress is actually pornstar Jodi Taylor under her real name.

Then, Amin is an obvious stand-in for young Kechiche. He even got his own sister to play his mother. Whenever Amin's not on screen, all the other characters are asking "Where's Amin?". When he's on screen, all the characters say, "You should date Amin, he's cool."

And you know what? After canto uno and canto due, which are around 5 hrs 20 in length, total, everyone is left on some random cliffhanger (well before the stage he would finally use material from the book he was supposedly adapting) that screams "I'm out of budget, I will complete this in a few months when the first film is released and is a massive hit." Which. Didn't. Happen.

Then, there's the elephant in the room, Mektoub, My Love: intermezzo, which was shot back to back with canto due, and was originally planned as a sequence from pt. 2. It was then moved in the chronology between the two main parts, but the "intermission" lasted three hours and a half. The only public screening in Cannes in 2019 was the thing that killed his career. It featured the lead actress receiving non-simulated oral sex during 12 minutes, and word gradually came out that:

1. She had only agreed to the scene with the condition that she would have some input over the use of her footage. Kechiche didn't give her any screening before the Cannes premiere, ghosted her for months before the premiere and had ultimately used all the raw footage, unedited. She quickly left after the press conference and didn't attend the screening, which set a few red lights in the press. That's why no distributor would touch the feature film.
2. "Agreed" is also too much of a word. He had asked the actress for an explicit sex scene for a while, then decided to shoot a sequence in a night club. And the sequence would never end. They shot weeks there. The set was very hot, due to the lights and the many extras, and he provided the cast on purpose with alcohol instead of water. That's how after weeks of pressure by the director who had turned Léa Seydoux and Adèle Exarchopoulos into stars, a young actress who was a newcomer accepted his request.
2. I didn't particularly enjoy Blue Is The Warmest Colour (not least because I resented the 3 hour run time) but I think to write it off as a soft-core lesbian male fantasy is just inaccurate. It's very obviously not that. Neither do I agree that Kechiche fighting back against Seydoux's public criticism of his filming methods says anything more remarkable than that he's an artist who defends himself and his work. This is a poor post.
  - This is one of those things that just keeps coming back around despite Lea Seydoux publicly denying that she hates Kechiche or that anything that happened constituted trauma. She reiterated how proud she was of the film, and she should be. It's incredible. I feel like anyone reducing the film to a porn-brained dude who had no honorable artistic intention with the work being created probably just didn't see it.

just kind of interesting! i agree with what you’re saying.

6. seems like the sort of director where art trumps all, and in his case, the artistic aspect involved a lot of sexually explicit scenes. There's something to be said about wanting to capture the perfect scene using someone else's body, with no concern for the actress, because art trumps all.
